author: @RobertLeyland
text:
Hey Robert, At 16:57 you discuss reversing the process to charge in parallel and discharge in series — stepping up the voltage.  I’m not sure that can work without an active component,  like a transistor… for each group.   In a diode multiplier we get a step up because we are using AC and the diode capacitor combination acts as a charge pump.   In this case it’s DC and passive components which seems unlikely to work.

I’d be happy to be shown where I’m wrong!

author: @Mars2152
text:
To enhance this fractal energy stack system using metals like copper, silver, and gold, here are some additional materials and techniques that could potentially boost the energy transmission and information transfer capabilities:

1. Rare Earth Elements (REEs): Elements such as neodymium, samarium, or yttrium could be incorporated into the stack to amplify magnetic properties and improve energy flow.


2. Superconductors: Incorporating high-temperature superconductors, such as yttrium barium copper oxide (YBCO), could help achieve room temperature energy transfer with minimal loss.


3. Graphene or Carbon Nanotubes: These carbon-based materials can conduct electricity and thermal energy efficiently, potentially increasing the system's overall energy transmission capabilities.


4. Metamaterials: Using specially engineered metamaterials, which have properties not found in naturally occurring materials, could allow for manipulation of electromagnetic waves, enhancing the efficiency of information transfer.


5. Tungsten or Platinum: These metals have high density and resistance to oxidation, which can be useful in stabilizing the structure and boosting thermal conductivity.


6. Phononic Crystals: These materials are designed to control phonon (vibrational energy) transmission and could be used to optimize the flow of thermal energy in the stack.


7. Nanostructured Surfaces: Applying nanoscale patterning on the metal surfaces can improve surface plasmon resonance, which boosts electromagnetic wave interactions and can be used for information transmission.


8. Quantum Dots: Integrating quantum dots can allow for precise control over electron behavior and photon emission, potentially enhancing information transmission in the stack.


9. Dielectric Materials: Placing dielectric layers like silicon dioxide or bismuth telluride between the metal layers may help manage energy flow and improve the system's thermal and electrical properties.


10. Piezoelectric or Pyroelectric Materials: Materials like quartz or lithium niobate could be added to the system to convert mechanical vibrations or thermal changes into electrical signals, offering another form of energy transfer.



Combining these materials with fractal stacking techniques could enhance the capabilities of your energy and information transmission system by manipulating electromagnetic fields, thermal energy, and quantum effects.

author: @KipIngram
text:
0:59 - Did you describe the capacitor voltage division backwards?  What's actually happening is that you have equal charge on the two capacitors.  If you put the same charge Q on a smaller capacitor, you'll get a higher voltage.  Q = C*V; keep Q the same, make C smaller, V has to be bigger.  So the larger capacitor will get the smaller voltage, and vice versa.
   
The charges match because there is no current path to the wire between the capacitors - the only way to get charge +Q onto one of them is to pull that charge from the other one.  The voltage source can only supply charge to / take charge from the outside plates of the two capacitors.

author: @BrentLeVasseur
text: You are an excellent teacher and I thank you for going to all the trouble you went to in order to illustrate this concept for us Youtube viewers! Ideally, this concept would be best applied not as a circuit board but as a single silicon wafer chip using present day chip technology. That’s the only way I think this idea would ever be economically feasible due to the economic costs of sourcing the components and actually assembling them together on say a production line. But for educational purposes, its brilliant as a proof of concept. As for your greater goal of harnessing environmental energy, I think Nikola Tesla did it with his resonant transformer design at Wardenclyff, which used the whole Earth as a capacitor plate. What you call “electrostatic” is really longitudinal dielectric. It’s a longitudinal standing wave in the Aether, which is formed by crossing two transverse magneto-electric currents at 180 degrees out of phase (as in a bifilar coil or bucking coil) so that they cancel out to form a single longitudinal standing wave in the Aether. On your mechanical rotary spark gap machine, which was creating high voltage impulses, the longitudinal wave is emitted at the discharge point of the spark gap for each cycle. Tesla’s hairpin circuit works in a similar fashion.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: also the advantage besides less parts in my design, is that it could well have been something Tesla was able to build with the technology that was available to him back in the day. just two mason jars and 3 coils (2 primary wound together and getting hooked up in reverse order and the 3rd one being  calculated out to the exit voltage you wanted, also the voltage going in can be controlled by adjusting the grounding plate closer or further away from the electroscope foils too.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: or you could use my design that is an electroscope with a grounding plate to allow self discharging once its fully charged and pass this through your first of two primary coils and to a second jar that has another unit in it and then that gets dumped through the reverse second primary coil before going to earth ground, thus you can then collect that power on the actual secondary as anything you wish based on coil configurations, and its AC sine wave too. at what ever voltage and thus what ever amperage you get from it after stepping it down or up, (depending on the amount of coils for the secondary vs the amount of coils for the two primaries wound in reverse, or at least hooked up in reverse order from each other to the dump point on the jar it goes to.)

author: @capnkirk5528
text:
I really want to encourage you to continue, because ...
On a different channel there was a discussion about Fermi's paradox and the hypothesis put forward by SOME people was that one civilization using us all the raw materials (metals / fossil fuels / easily-accessible radioactives / He4 / etc) would PREVENT any successor civilization from developing. 
I disagree with that hypothesis because ... NECESSITY is the mother of invention and those civilizations will find a way.
Which leads to the corollary - plenty is the father of inactivity. We haven't invented electrostatic science YET because ...WE DON'T NEED IT (YET). We found electromagnetism, steam, fossil fuels, etc first. Not a lot of motivation to look for more (the fossil fuel people are violently opposing us looking for more so they can stay RICH 😬).
BUT ... any solution for harvesting environmental energy in a meaningful way will be HUGE. After all, that is what a PV panel does - harvest energy that was just lying around. Go outside on a sunny day and there is a kw/sq m to just pick up; it costs nothing once you have the pieces.
I would say that it's better than 90% likely that there is something along this line in the electrostatic "arena" and THAT solution may be as world-changing as the PV panel (and possibly not as inconvenient).
This isn't "perpetual motion" or "infinite energy", it's POTENTIALLY very similar to the "infinite energy" I get from a Renology panel.

author: @nigeljohnson9820
text:
With all those diodes it's surprising that the fractal works out to be more efficient. Presumable the total series Vf of the diodes in any charging path must be lower using a fractal configuration. It would be interesting to compare the diodes charge path in a Cockcroft multplier with the fractal equivalent.
Isn't the absolute voltage of the high voltage limited by the PIV of the first three diodes in series? To increase the PIV, it is usual to put more diodes in series, which reduces the efficiency.

replies.author: @dynamicresistance
replies.text: so the first 3 diodes would need to be extreme... or... in massive parallel design?

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text:
@dynamicresistance I have since found some diodes used for microwaves, they are rated with a PIV of 12kV @ 450mA. That gives 36kV input voltage for the first three diodes in the basic building block. ( The Peak inverse voltage limitation  makes it necessary to put more diodes in series to handle the high voltage without breakdown, that generally increases the Vf losses.)
My comment on the Cockcroft multiplier was ill judged, as the fractional design is for DC and not an AC supply. The diodes just switching between the series charging connection and the parallel discharge connection. I downloaded the cited document on piezoelectric energy recovery that use the fractal design. The energy losses are associated with the Vf of the diodes, and how many end up in series to get down to the required output. It is an interesting way to get from a high DC voltage at low current, to a lower DC voltage at a higher current. It may be a way to scavenge useful energy from static electricity, such as that associated with aerial space charge.

author: @ViaExplore
text: The only issue I see with this, is that the high voltage is always sitting on 3 diodes in serial connection. Therefore their reverse voltage treshold (zener voltage) has to be at least 1/2 of the HV input. The current and voltage dynamics during charge would be really interesting to see. For sort period of time, the diodes are doing really crazy stuff, as the holes in PN junction are quite slow. If one diode is faster then other one, the full HV can be observed on any od these 3 diodes which could potentialy destroy it (like what ESD discharge does in some cases). I think the HV rectifiers are already addressing this issue when you try to serial connect them to increase the "reverse voltage protection".  Or am I wrong?

replies.author: @deang5622
replies.text:
The maximum reverse voltage a diode can handle is not the zener voltage.
The zener voltage is the designed voltage produced by a zener diode. Ok, that happens to be a diode in reverse conduction but the point is the zener voltage is applied to a zener diode only and not to other types of diodes.

author: @matthewgreen1152
text: I don't always use magnets, I use magnetism a lot. Electromagnets with motor brushes in diy custom transformers allow you to step out variously, with the availability of stepping up without overheating by returning the resistance to the charge controller(s) with batteries. I had 2banks, 1 day and 1 night, each having inverters . At night the lights were charging the day bank. Bigger appliances  taking both banks with dedicated alone time to use. The step out transformer was beyond the usage built for.  With a few more advancements by necessity, the future should be a leap ahead, after the so slow of a pace it seemed to be backwards in direction is finally getting less in appearance.

author: @Kezat
text: One main reason capacitors are not used for power conversation is the energy stored will always be half the supplied energy. With a reference frame larger then a single ideal capacitor, half the charging energy will be dissipated in the resistance of the charging circuit. QV/2.

replies.author: @TnTOmnibus
replies.text: that's not quite right mate - capacitors are used all over the place for conversion and storage - you are right in some circumstances it's not a good idea but in others it works well

author: @gerryjamesedwards1227
text: 25:40 Just in case it's useful to someone doing this with a large number, you can buy packs of 4-pin PWM PC-fan extension cables for not very much at all. These have the same colour wires as used here and breadboard-compatible wire fits neatly into the sockets on either end, if female-female, or you could solder diodes directly between pins on male connectors.

author: @travismoore7849
text: What if you replaced the diodes with diode bridges with capacitors across the input in series on the ac side and in parallel on the dc side so the input of the diode bridges are in series and the output is in parallel to save room on the circuit board?

replies.author: @travismoore7849
replies.text: @thekaxmax I guess you just get the cheep diode bridges for 10 pack with 4 pin @ 1 amp at 1000v. Only cast 4.78.  Digi key sells them for 14.62 for 100 ; too high?

replies.author: @TnTOmnibus
replies.text: go for it mate - give it a go

author: @kwinterburn
text: What is the discharge  current path from the lowest charged capacitor ?

replies.author: @TheBaconWizard
replies.text: There isn't a lowest charged capacitor, the whole point is that they are all the same. That being said, if for some reason you used a different cap value somewhere, the path wouldn't change because of it, it would still be as shown in the video.

replies.author: @TnTOmnibus
replies.text: there isn't one mate

author: @kwinterburn
text: So in reality each diode string has a diode and capacitor string across each diode so you apply high voltage which charges up all the capacitors to 1/2 of the voltage the individual diode sees and so on so 10kv becomes 5 kv then 2.5 then 1200v then 600 then 300 then 150 then 75 then 38 then 19 then 10 ish  so 11 stages divided the voltage by 1000 ish then when you remove the high voltage the voltage now very much smaller in the capacitors , discharges using the bypass diodes to the output , all very well and dandy I would just be wondering about quite how useful it would be to be honest ,

replies.author: @Scarecrow99999-j
replies.text: Capacitors can be printed with conductive ink on paper, and so can a Wimshurst high voltage generator.  I wonder if diodes could be?  If so, it might lead to a very cheap way to produce a hand crank generator to charge a phone.    Used in reverse a lightweight fractal capacitor could replace the heavy transformer in a microwave oven.  Scaled up they could replace transformers in the power grid.  I can see lots of potential uses.
